papers.title: Hyper Velocity Stars and the Restricted Parabolic 3-body Problem
papers.author_names: Re'em Sari Shiho Kobayashi Elena M. Rossi

abstract.text: Motivated by detections of hypervelocity stars that may originate from the Galactic Center, we revist the problem of a binary disruption by a passage near a much more massive point mass. The six order of magnitude mass ratio between the Galactic Center black hole and the binary stars allows us to formulate the problem in the restricted parabolic three-body approximation. In this framework, results can be simply rescaled in terms of binary masses, its initial separation and binary-to-black hole mass ratio. Consequently, an advantage over the full three-body calculation is that a much smaller set of simulations is needed to explore the relevant parameter space. Contrary to previous claims, we show that, upon binary disruption, the lighter star does not remain preferentially bound to the black hole. In fact, it is ejected exactly in 50% of the cases. Nonetheless, lighter objects have higher ejection velocities, since the energy distribution is independent of mass. Focusing on the planar case, we provide the probability distributions for disruption of circular binaries and for the ejection energy. We show that even binaries that penetrate deeply into the tidal sphere of the black hole are not doomed to disruption, but survive in 20% of the cases. Nor do these deep encounters produce the highest ejection energies, which are instead obtained for binaries arriving to 0.1-0.5 of the tidal radius in a prograde orbit. Interestingly, such deep-reaching binaries separate widely after penetrating the tidal radius, but always approach each other again on their way out from the black hole.[shortened]

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper revisits the mechanism by which binary stars can be disrupted by a supermassive black hole at the Galactic Center, using a restricted parabolic three-body approximation due to the extreme mass ratio (10⁶). It shows that the lighter star is ejected in exactly 50% of cases, contrary to prior assumptions, and that ejection energies peak at prograde orbits near 0.1–0.5 tidal radius, not deep penetrations. The ejection energy is robust to initial binary separation and depends only on minimal separation during disruption, enabling scalable analytic predictions without full three-body simulations.

source_fields.title: Hyper Velocity Stars and the Restricted Parabolic 3-body Problem

source_fields.sections_text: 5. Results: Binaries with penetration factor $D \\ll 1$ are often not disrupted, with a disruption probability saturating at $\\sim 80\\%$ for $D < 10^{-1}$, and surviving binaries tend to tighten, with final semi-major axes $a_f < a$, while final ejected star energies depend primarily on the effective binary phase $\\phi$ and are well-described by radial orbit limits, showing survival in specific $\\phi$ ranges. 1. Introduction: This paper novelly demonstrates that dynamical breakup of a binary star near the Galactic Center, via tidal forces from the supermassive black hole, can produce hypervelocity stars with velocities up to thousands of km/s—solving the long-standing problem of how faint B-type stars achieve such extreme ejection speeds through a mechanism beyond standard binary interactions. 7. Ejection velocities: The average ejection velocity of stars from binary systems near a supermassive black hole reaches a saturation value depending on the penetration factor or minimum separation, with prograde orbits yielding slightly higher velocities than retrograde ones and harder binaries coalescing before achieving high speeds. 6. Finite Stellar Sizes: This section shows that the final ejection energy of stars in binary systems is highly sensitive to the minimum separation $R_{\\rm min}$ (sum of stellar radii) rather than initial separation, with maximal energies reaching approximately 1.64 (prograde) and 1.60 (retrograde) times $Gm_1m_2/R_{\\rm min}(M/m)^{1/3}$. 4. Numerical Verifications: This section demonstrates that the approximated equations accurately reproduce the evolution of binary stars disrupted by a supermassive black hole, with high precision and computational efficiency compared to full three-body simulations. 8. Discussion and Conclusions: The study finds that tidally disrupted binary systems can produce hyper velocity stars with ejection velocities up to ~7000 km/s, significantly exceeding previous estimates, with maximum velocities reaching 0.9×v_esc×(M/m)^1/6 and up to 1.8× higher than prior models for large mass ratios. 2. The parabolic restricted 3 body problem: This section derives the motion of a binary system in the parabolic restricted three-body problem, showing that the relative motion of the two bodies is governed by a dimensionless equation of motion that depends on the parabolic trajectory of the binary's center of mass and the gravitational interaction with the central mass. 3. Deep Penetrators and the radial restricted 3 body problem: This section shows that despite initial appearances, deep penetrators in the radial restricted three-body problem do not achieve unbounded energies, as solutions exhibit bounded behavior due to tidal forces and the inherent constraints of the system.

abstract: Motivated by detections of hypervelocity stars that may originate from the Galactic Center, we revist the problem of a binary disruption by a passage near a much more massive point mass.
The six order of magnitude mass ratio between the Galactic Center black hole and the binary stars allows us to formulate the problem in the restricted parabolic three-body approximation.
title: Hyper Velocity Stars and the Restricted Parabolic 3-body Problem

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper on binary disruption mechanism using restricted parabolic three-body approximation; mentions hypervelocity stars as motivation but presents no observational data or object lists
